original_hypothesis.title: NoyaFeeReceiver.withdrawShares Creates Permanent Share Lock Without Cancellation Path
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: NoyaFeeReceiver.withdrawShares calls AccountingManager.withdraw(amount, receiver) which queues a withdrawal and increments withdrawRequestsByAddress[NoyaFeeReceiver] to lock shares. There is no mechanism to cancel a queued withdrawal. If the manager never processes the withdrawal (or it gets stuck in an incomplete group), the shares are permanently locked, preventing the fee receiver from transferring or burning them. The _update override enforces this lock on all transfers.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The withdrawal queue has no cancellation function. Once shares are locked via withdrawRequestsByAddress, they can only be unlocked through executeWithdraw. If the withdraw group processing is delayed, paused, or the amount is too small to justify a group, the fee receiver's shares become permanently unusable.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdraw() increments withdrawRequestsByAddress with no corresponding cancellation function; _update at line 151 enforces balanceOf >= amount + withdrawRequestsByAddress

preconditions: Manager does not process the withdrawal group to completion
Contract remains paused or group processing is indefinitely delayed

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: withdrawRequestsByAddress[NoyaFeeReceiver] += share with no decrement path except executeWithdraw
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Fee receiver shares become permanently locked, reducing effective circulating supply
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Locked fee shares cannot be burned (reducing supply) or withdrawn (collecting fees), creating permanent value leakage

economic_questions: How much fee value could be permanently locked?
What conditions prevent withdrawal group completion?
Can the emergency role rescue these locked shares?
validation_requirements: Queue a withdrawal from NoyaFeeReceiver and verify share lock
Attempt to burn or transfer locked shares and confirm revert
Check if any recovery path exists

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: N/A - DoS/value lock, not profit extraction

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Typical fee share amounts and withdrawal timing

validation_result.impact_summary: NoyaFeeReceiver (and any user) that queues a withdrawal via AccountingManager.withdraw() has shares permanently locked in withdrawRequestsByAddress with no cancellation mechanism. The only decrement path is through the full executeWithdraw pipeline.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis correctly identifies that AccountingManager.withdraw() increments withdrawRequestsByAddress[msg.sender] and there is no corresponding cancellation function. The _update override at line 151 enforces balanceOf(from) >= amount + withdrawRequestsByAddress[from] for all transfers (including burns). The only code path that decrements withdrawRequestsByAddress is in executeWithdraw. If the withdrawal processing pipeline (calculateWithdrawShares -> startCurrentWithdrawGroup -> fulfillCurrentWithdrawGroup -> executeWithdraw) is delayed, paused, or disrupted at any stage, the shares remain locked indefinitely. While the emergency role can act as manager to process withdrawals, this creates an unnecessary operational dependency and single point of failure. The NoyaFeeReceiver is particularly affected because it uses withdrawShares() and burnShares() - once shares are locked by a withdrawal request, burnShares() will also revert due to the _update check.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal is accurate. Lines 193-208 of AccountingManager.sol show withdraw() incrementing withdrawRequestsByAddress with no cancellation counterpart. Line 151 shows the _update override enforcing the lock. A thorough search of the entire AccountingManager contract confirms there is no cancelWithdraw or similar function. The only decrement is at the executeWithdraw function where withdrawRequestsByAddress[data.owner] -= shares occurs.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Increments withdrawRequestsByAddress with no corresponding cancellation function anywhere in the contract. The only decrement path is through executeWithdraw, which requires the full withdrawal processing pipeline to complete.
Enforces the share lock on ALL transfers including burns. Once withdrawRequestsByAddress is incremented, the user cannot transfer or burn those shares until executeWithdraw processes the request.

NoyaFeeReceiver calls withdraw with msg.sender being the NoyaFeeReceiver contract itself, locking shares in withdrawRequestsByAddress[NoyaFeeReceiver]. After this call, burnShares() will also fail for the locked amount because _burn triggers _update which checks the lock.

vulnerable_code_snippets.code_snippet:
// Only place withdrawRequestsByAddress is decremented:
withdrawRequestsByAddress[data.owner] -= shares;
vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: This is the ONLY code path in the entire contract that decrements withdrawRequestsByAddress. No cancellation alternative exists.

calling_sequence: 2. AccountingManager.withdraw(amount, receiver) called with msg.sender = NoyaFeeReceiver
5. _update override now blocks transfers/burns for locked amount
6. If NoyaFeeReceiver.burnShares(amount) is later called, _burn -> _update -> REVERT
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: NoyaFeeReceiver.withdrawShares -> AccountingManager.withdraw -> withdrawRequestsByAddress incremented -> _update enforces lock -> No cancellation path exists -> shares locked until executeWithdraw processes the request through the full pipeline (calculateWithdrawShares -> startCurrentWithdrawGroup -> fulfillCurrentWithdrawGroup -> executeWithdraw)

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment configuration affects this issue. The missing cancellation mechanism is inherent to the contract code.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Scenario 1: NoyaFeeReceiver owner calls withdrawShares(1000). The manager is busy or the withdrawal amount is small. The shares are now locked. The owner then tries to call burnShares(1000) to reduce supply instead - this reverts because _update checks balanceOf(NoyaFeeReceiver) >= 1000 + withdrawRequestsByAddress[NoyaFeeReceiver]. Scenario 2: The contract gets paused (emergencyStop) after a withdrawal is queued but before processing. All processing functions have whenNotPaused modifier, so the withdrawal cannot be processed while paused. The shares remain locked until unpause + full processing pipeline completion. Scenario 3: Manager calls calculateWithdrawShares for the group but the withdrawal amount calculated to 0 (edge case with rounding). The request still occupies the queue with locked shares but provides 0 base tokens on execution.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM severity. The NoyaFeeReceiver contract specifically uses both withdrawShares() and burnShares(). Once withdrawShares() is called, the locked shares cannot be burned until the full withdrawal pipeline completes. This creates an operational dependency where fee collection (via withdrawal to base tokens) blocks fee burning (share supply reduction). For regular users, the same issue applies - queued withdrawals cannot be cancelled, and shares are locked until the manager processes them. The impact is amplified if the contract is paused or the manager is unresponsive.
recommended_mitigations: Add a cancelWithdraw function that allows users to cancel their pending withdrawal requests and decrement withdrawRequestsByAddress accordingly
Ensure the cancellation function can only cancel requests that have not yet been included in a started withdraw group (i.e., queue index >= currentWithdrawGroup.lastId or before the group is started)
Consider adding a timeout mechanism where withdrawal requests automatically become cancellable after a certain period if not processed
For NoyaFeeReceiver specifically, consider separating the withdrawal queue lock from the burn mechanism so that burnShares is not blocked by pending withdrawals
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy AccountingManager and NoyaFeeReceiver. 2. Mint fee shares to NoyaFeeReceiver via collectManagementFees or collectPerformanceFees. 3. Call NoyaFeeReceiver.withdrawShares(X). 4. Verify withdrawRequestsByAddress[NoyaFeeReceiver] == X. 5. Attempt NoyaFeeReceiver.burnShares(1) and verify it reverts with NoyaAccounting_INSUFFICIENT_FUNDS. 6. Search entire contract for any function that decrements withdrawRequestsByAddress - confirm only executeWithdraw does. 7. Pause the contract and verify the withdrawal cannot be processed or cancelled.
uncertainties: The emergency role can always unpause and process withdrawals as manager, so 'permanent' lock requires emergency key loss or compromise
In practice, managers are expected to process withdrawals regularly, so the lock may be temporary in normal operation
The severity depends on operational practices and whether the manager processes all withdrawal groups promptly
additional_testing_needed: Test the interaction between withdrawShares and burnShares on NoyaFeeReceiver to confirm burns are blocked
Test what happens if a withdrawal request amount rounds to 0 in calculateWithdrawShares
Test the full withdrawal pipeline with NoyaFeeReceiver as the requester to confirm end-to-end flow works when the manager cooperates
Test edge case where contract is paused mid-withdrawal-group processing
